questions.specification:
A particle moves along a straight line with displacement, $s(t)$, in meters, from a fixed point at time $t$ seconds, modeled by

$$s(t)=5+4 e^{-0.03 t} \sin \left(\frac{\pi}{10} t\right)-3 \cos \left(\frac{\pi}{10} t+\frac{\pi}{6}\right)$$

for $0 \leq t \leq 30$, where angles are measured in radians. The particle is at risk of collision when $|s(t)| \geq 6$.

- Correct expression for $v(t)$: $$v(t) = 4 e^{-0.03 t} \left[ -0.03 \sin \left(\frac{\pi}{10} t\right) + \frac{\pi}{10} \cos \left(\frac{\pi}{10} t\right) \right] + \frac{3\pi}{10} \sin \left(\frac{\pi}{10} t+\frac{\pi}{6}\right)$$ <marks>A2</marks>

- Attempt to maximize acceleration $a(t) = v'(t)$ numerically (e.g., graphing $a(t)$ and finding max) <marks>M1</marks>

- Maximum acceleration $\approx 0.764 \text{ m s}^{-2}$ <marks>A1</marks>

questions.specification:
A tidal power generator produces power, $P(t)$, in megawatts, modeled by

$$
P(t)=10+8 \sin \left(\frac{\pi}{6} t\right)+6 e^{-0.01 t} \cos \left(\frac{\pi}{6} t-\frac{\pi}{4}\right)
$$

where $t$ is the time in hours since midnight, for $0 \leq t \leq 48$. The generator is considered operational when $P(t) \geq 12$.

parts.content: Find the initial power output at $t=0$.
parts.markscheme:
- Calculate the value at $t=0$: $P(0)=10+8 \sin (0)+6 e^{0} \cos \left(-\frac{\pi}{4}\right)$ <marks>M1</marks>

- State the result: $P(0) = 10 + 3\sqrt{2} \approx 14.24 \text{ MW}$ <marks>A1</marks>

2 marks total

steps.title: Identify the initial condition
steps.content: To find the initial power output, we need to evaluate the function $P(t)$ at the starting time. In mathematical models, "initial" refers to the time when $t = 0$.

highlights.text: Find the initial power output at $t=0$.

steps.title: Substitute zero for time
steps.content:
We substitute $t=0$ into the given power function expression:

$$P(0) = 10 + 8 \sin\left(\frac{\pi}{6} \cdot 0\right) + 6 e^{-0.01 \cdot 0} \cos\left(\frac{\pi}{6} \cdot 0 - \frac{\pi}{4}\right)$$

This simplifies the terms inside the sine, cosine, and exponential functions.

steps.title: Evaluate individual terms
steps.content:
Now we evaluate each part of the expression using our knowledge of trigonometric ratios and powers:

1. $\sin(0) = 0$
2. $e^0 = 1$ (any non-zero number to the power of 0 is 1)
3. $\cos\left(-\frac{\pi}{4}\right) = \frac{\sqrt{2}}{2}$

Substituting these values back into the equation gives us:

$$P(0) = 10 + 8(0) + 6(1)\left(\frac{\sqrt{2}}{2}\right)$$

steps.title: Calculate the final result
steps.content:
Simplifying the expression leads to the exact value:

$$P(0) = 10 + 3\sqrt{2}$$

Using a calculator for the final numerical value:

$$P(0) \approx 14.2426\dots$$

Rounding to the standard three significant figures for IB Mathematics, the initial power output is $14.2$ MW.

parts.content: Determine the period of the oscillatory components.
parts.markscheme:
- Identify the angular frequency $\omega = \frac{\pi}{6}$ <marks>M1</marks>

- Calculate the period: $T = \frac{2 \pi}{\frac{\pi}{6}}=12$ hours <marks>A1</marks>

2 marks total

steps.title: Identify the oscillatory terms
steps.content:
To find the period, we first need to look at the trigonometric parts of the function $P(t)$. The function contains both a sine and a cosine term, which represent the oscillatory (repeating) components of the power generation.

$$P(t)=10+8 \sin \left(\frac{\pi}{6} t\right)+6 e^{-0.01 t} \cos \left(\frac{\pi}{6} t-\frac{\pi}{4}\right)$$

Notice that even though the cosine term is multiplied by an exponential decay ($e^{-0.01t}$), the "frequency" of the oscillation is determined by the coefficient inside the trigonometric functions.

steps.title: Extract the angular frequency
steps.content:
For any sinusoidal function in the form $y = a \sin(Bt + c) + d$, the coefficient $B$ is known as the angular frequency. Looking at our model, both trigonometric terms have the same coefficient for $t$:

$$B = \frac{\pi}{6}$$

In IB Mathematics, this is often the first step to earning marks in modeling questions.

steps.title: Apply the period formula
steps.content:
The period $T$ is the time it takes for one full cycle to complete. It is related to the angular frequency $B$ by the formula:

$$T = \frac{2\pi}{B}$$

Now, we substitute our value $B = \frac{\pi}{6}$ into this equation:

$$T = \frac{2\pi}{\frac{\pi}{6}}$$

steps.title: Calculate the final period
steps.content:
To simplify the fraction, we multiply by the reciprocal:

$$\begin{aligned} T &= 2\pi \times \frac{6}{\pi} \\ T &= 12 \end{aligned}$$

So, the period of the oscillatory components is $12$ hours. This means the tidal cycle repeats every $12$ hours.

gdc.instructions: Calculate (2 * π) / (π / 6) in the run-matrix menu.

ti84.instructions: Enter (2 * π) / (π / 6) on the main screen.

desmos.instructions: Type (2 * pi) / (pi / 6) into a new cell.

calculator.description: Verify the period calculation.

steps.title: Input the function
steps.content:
To determine the period using graphical analysis, first enter the power function $P(t)$ into your graphing calculator. 

$$P(t)=10+8 \sin \left(\frac{\pi}{6} t\right)+6 e^{-0.01 t} \cos \left(\frac{\pi}{6} t-\frac{\pi}{4}\right)$$

Ensure your calculator is in **Radians** mode, as the oscillatory components involve $\pi$. Set your viewing window for $t$ (x-axis) from $0$ to $24$ to see at least two full cycles.

desmos.instructions: Type the function into the expression bar. Use the wrench icon to set the x-axis from 0 to 48 and y-axis from 0 to 25.

calculator.description: Enter the function and set the window.

steps.title: Find consecutive peaks
steps.content:
The period of an oscillating function is the horizontal distance between two consecutive peaks (maxima). Use the 'Maximum' tool on your GDC to find the $t$-coordinates of the first two peaks.

From the graph, we find:
- First peak: $t_1 \approx 2.35$
- Second peak: $t_2 \approx 14.35$

Notice that the height of the peaks decreases slightly over time due to the $e^{-0.01t}$ damping term, but the horizontal spacing remains constant.

gdc.instructions: Press [F5] (G-Solv) then [F2] (MAX). Use the arrow keys to toggle between the first and second maximum points found.

ti84.instructions: Press [2nd] [TRACE] (CALC), select '4: maximum'. Move the cursor to the left and right of the first peak for 'Left Bound' and 'Right Bound', then press [ENTER] on 'Guess'. Repeat for the second peak.

desmos.instructions: Click on the graph near the first and second peaks. Gray dots will appear at the maxima; click them to see the coordinates.

calculator.description: Find the maximum points on the graph.

steps.title: Calculate the period
steps.content:
Subtract the $t$-coordinates of the two consecutive peaks to find the period ($T$):

$$\begin{aligned} T &= t_2 - t_1 \\ T &= 14.35 - 2.35 \\ T &= 12 \text{ hours} \end{aligned}$$

This matches the theoretical period derived from the angular frequency $\omega = \frac{\pi}{6}$ using the formula $$T = \frac{2\pi}{\omega}$$ from the data booklet.

parts.content: Express the oscillatory part of $P(t)$ as a single trigonometric function with time-dependent amplitude and phase shift.
parts.markscheme:
- Identify the oscillatory part: $8 \sin \left(\frac{\pi}{6} t\right)+6 e^{-0.01 t} \cos \left(\frac{\pi}{6} t-\frac{\pi}{4}\right)$ <marks>M1</marks>

- Expand using harmonic addition or phasor components: $A(t)=\sqrt{(8 + 6 e^{-0.01 t}\sin\frac{\pi}{4})^2 + (6 e^{-0.01 t} \cos \frac{\pi}{4})^{2}}$ <marks>A1</marks>

- Identify the phase shift: $\phi(t)=\tan ^{-1}\left(\frac{6 e^{-0.01 t} \cos \frac{\pi}{4}}{8+6 e^{-0.01 t} \sin \frac{\pi}{4}}\right)$ <marks>A1</marks>

- Simplify $A(t)$: $A(t)=\sqrt{64+36 e^{-0.02 t}+48\sqrt{2} e^{-0.01 t}}$ <marks>A1</marks>

- Simplify $\phi(t)$: $\phi(t)=\tan ^{-1}\left(\frac{3 \sqrt{2} e^{-0.01 t}}{8+3 \sqrt{2} e^{-0.01 t}}\right)$ <marks>A1</marks>

5 marks total

steps.title: Identify the oscillatory part
steps.content:
The power function $P(t)$ is made up of a constant base and an oscillatory component. The oscillatory part consists of all terms that vary with time $t$ via sine or cosine functions.

Oscillatory part:
$$8 \sin \left(\frac{\pi}{6} t\right)+6 e^{-0.01 t} \cos \left(\frac{\pi}{6} t-\frac{\pi}{4}\right)$$

steps.title: Expand the cosine term
steps.content:
Using the cosine difference identity $\cos(A-B) = \cos(A)\cos(B) + \sin(A)\sin(B)$ (where $A = \frac{\pi}{6}t$ and $B = \frac{\pi}{4}$), we expand the second term:

$$\cos\left(\frac{\pi}{6}t - \frac{\pi}{4}\right) = \cos\left(\frac{\pi}{6}t\right)\cos\left(\frac{\pi}{4}\right) + \sin\left(\frac{\pi}{6}t\right)\sin\left(\frac{\pi}{4}\right)$$

Since $\cos\left(\frac{\pi}{4}\right) = \frac{\sqrt{2}}{2}$ and $\sin\left(\frac{\pi}{4}\right) = \frac{\sqrt{2}}{2}$:

$$\frac{\sqrt{2}}{2} \cos\left(\frac{\pi}{6}t\right) + \frac{\sqrt{2}}{2} \sin\left(\frac{\pi}{6}t\right)$$

steps.title: Collect sine and cosine coefficients
steps.content:
Substitute the expansion back into the oscillatory expression and group the terms for $\sin\left(\frac{\pi}{6}t\right)$ and $\cos\left(\frac{\pi}{6}t\right)$:

$$\begin{aligned} \text{Osc. Part} &= 8 \sin \left(\frac{\pi}{6} t\right) + 6 e^{-0.01 t} \left[ \frac{\sqrt{2}}{2} \cos \left(\frac{\pi}{6} t\right) + \frac{\sqrt{2}}{2} \sin \left(\frac{\pi}{6} t\right) \right] \\ &= 8 \sin \left(\frac{\pi}{6} t\right) + 3\sqrt{2} e^{-0.01 t} \cos \left(\frac{\pi}{6} t\right) + 3\sqrt{2} e^{-0.01 t} \sin \left(\frac{\pi}{6} t\right) \\ &= (8 + 3\sqrt{2} e^{-0.01 t}) \sin \left(\frac{\pi}{6} t\right) + (3\sqrt{2} e^{-0.01 t}) \cos \left(\frac{\pi}{6} t\right) \end{aligned}$$

steps.title: Find time-dependent amplitude
steps.content:
The expression is now in the form $B \sin(\theta) + A \cos(\theta)$, which can be written as $A(t) \sin(\theta + \phi(t))$. The amplitude $A(t)$ is given by $\sqrt{A^2 + B^2}$:

$$\begin{aligned} A(t) &= \sqrt{(8 + 3\sqrt{2} e^{-0.01 t})^2 + (3\sqrt{2} e^{-0.01 t})^2} \\ &= \sqrt{(64 + 48\sqrt{2}e^{-0.01 t} + 18e^{-0.02 t}) + (18e^{-0.02 t})} \\ &= \sqrt{64 + 36 e^{-0.02 t} + 48\sqrt{2} e^{-0.01 t}} \end{aligned}$$

steps.title: Identify the phase shift
steps.content:
The phase shift $\phi(t)$ is found using the ratio of the coefficients: $\phi(t) = \arctan\left(\frac{\text{coefficient of cosine}}{\text{coefficient of sine}}\right)$.

$$\phi(t) = \arctan\left(\frac{3\sqrt{2} e^{-0.01 t}}{8 + 3\sqrt{2} e^{-0.01 t}}\right)$$

Combining these, the oscillatory part is $A(t) \sin\left(\frac{\pi}{6}t + \phi(t)\right)$ where $A(t) = \sqrt{64 + 36 e^{-0.02 t} + 48\sqrt{2} e^{-0.01 t}}$ and $\phi(t) = \arctan\left(\frac{3 \sqrt{2} e^{-0.01 t}}{8 + 3 \sqrt{2} e^{-0.01 t}}\right)$.

steps.title: Identify the oscillatory part
steps.content:
To start, we need to isolate the terms in the power function $P(t)$ that cause the periodic oscillation. These are the trigonometric terms.

The constant term $+10$ represents the average power level, while the rest describes the variation over time:

$$f(t) = 8 \sin \left(\frac{\pi}{6} t\right) + 6 e^{-0.01 t} \cos \left(\frac{\pi}{6} t - \frac{\pi}{4}\right)$$

Our goal is to combine these into a single expression of the form $A(t) \sin\left(\frac{\pi}{6} t + \phi(t)\right)$.

steps.title: Convert to consistent basis
steps.content:
To use the phasor addition method easily, we want both terms to be in terms of $\sin$. Using the identity $\cos(\theta) = \sin\left(\theta + \frac{\pi}{2}\right)$, we can rewrite the second term:

$$\begin{aligned} 6 e^{-0.01 t} \cos \left(\frac{\pi}{6} t - \frac{\pi}{4}\right) &= 6 e^{-0.01 t} \sin \left(\frac{\pi}{6} t - \frac{\pi}{4} + \frac{\pi}{2}\right) \\ &= 6 e^{-0.01 t} \sin \left(\frac{\pi}{6} t + \frac{\pi}{4}\right) \end{aligned}$$

Now the expression is:
$$f(t) = 8 \sin \left(\frac{\pi}{6} t\right) + 6 e^{-0.01 t} \sin \left(\frac{\pi}{6} t + \frac{\pi}{4}\right)$$

This is a standard problem in **Topic 1: Number and Algebra (AHL 1.13)**, where we apply complex number methods (phasors) to sinusoidal functions.

steps.title: Define the phasors
steps.content:
We represent each sine wave as a vector (phasor) where the length is the amplitude and the angle is the phase shift relative to $\sin\left(\frac{\pi}{6}t\right)$.

- **Phasor 1** (from $8 \sin\left(\frac{\pi}{6}t\right)$): Magnitude $r_1 = 8$, Phase $\theta_1 = 0$.
- **Phasor 2** (from $6 e^{-0.01 t} \sin\left(\frac{\pi}{6}t + \frac{\pi}{4}\right)$): Magnitude $r_2(t) = 6 e^{-0.01 t}$, Phase $\theta_2 = \frac{\pi}{4}$.

We now calculate the total horizontal ($X$) and vertical ($Y$) components of the resultant phasor:

$$\begin{aligned} X &= r_1 \cos(0) + r_2(t) \cos\left(\frac{\pi}{4}\right) \\ Y &= r_1 \sin(0) + r_2(t) \sin\left(\frac{\pi}{4}\right) \end{aligned}$$

steps.title: Sum the components
steps.content:
Since $\cos\left(\frac{\pi}{4}\right) = \sin\left(\frac{\pi}{4}\right) = \frac{\sqrt{2}}{2}$, we substitute the values to find the components of the resultant phasor:

$$\begin{aligned} X &= 8 + 6 e^{-0.01 t} \left(\frac{\sqrt{2}}{2}\right) = 8 + 3\sqrt{2} e^{-0.01 t} \\ Y &= 0 + 6 e^{-0.01 t} \left(\frac{\sqrt{2}}{2}\right) = 3\sqrt{2} e^{-0.01 t} \end{aligned}$$

steps.title: Find the time-dependent amplitude
steps.content:
The amplitude $A(t)$ is the magnitude of the resultant phasor, found using the Pythagorean theorem $A(t) = \sqrt{X^2 + Y^2}$:

$$\begin{aligned} A(t)^2 &= (8 + 3\sqrt{2} e^{-0.01 t})^2 + (3\sqrt{2} e^{-0.01 t})^2 \\ &= (64 + 48\sqrt{2} e^{-0.01 t} + 18 e^{-0.02 t}) + (18 e^{-0.02 t}) \\ &= 64 + 36 e^{-0.02 t} + 48\sqrt{2} e^{-0.01 t} \end{aligned}$$

Taking the square root gives the amplitude:
$$A(t) = \sqrt{64 + 36 e^{-0.02 t} + 48\sqrt{2} e^{-0.01 t}}$$

steps.title: Find the phase shift
steps.content:
The phase shift $\phi(t)$ is the angle of the resultant phasor, calculated as $\tan^{-1}\left(\frac{Y}{X}\right)$:

$$\phi(t) = \tan^{-1} \left( \frac{3\sqrt{2} e^{-0.01 t}}{8 + 3\sqrt{2} e^{-0.01 t}} \right)$$

Putting it all together, the oscillatory part is:
$$P_{osc}(t) = A(t) \sin\left(\frac{\pi}{6} t + \phi(t)\right)$$ 
where $A(t)$ and $\phi(t)$ are the functions derived above.

parts.content: Find the first time the generator is not operational ($P(t)<12$) after being operational.
parts.markscheme:
- Set $P(t)=12$ to find the crossing point <marks>M1</marks>

- Sketch the graph or use a numerical solver for $10+8 \sin \left(\frac{\pi}{6} t\right)+6 e^{-0.01 t} \cos \left(\frac{\pi}{6} t-\frac{\pi}{4}\right) = 12$ <marks>A1</marks>

- Find the first time $t > 0$ where $P(t)$ crosses 12 downwards <marks>A1</marks>

- State the answer: $t \approx 5.08$ hours <marks>A1</marks>

4 marks total

steps.title: Define the operational condition
steps.content:
The question defines the generator as operational when $P(t) \geq 12$. To find when it **stops** being operational, we need to find the first value of $t > 0$ where the power output $P(t)$ drops below 12 megawatts.

Mathematically, we are looking for the first intersection point where the graph of $P(t)$ crosses the line $y = 12$ while moving downwards.

highlights.text: Find the first time the generator is not operational ($P(t)<12$) after being operational

steps.title: Graph the functions
steps.content:
Using your Graphing Display Calculator (GDC), plot the following two functions:

$$y_1 = 10 + 8 \sin \left(\frac{\pi}{6} t\right) + 6 e^{-0.01 t} \cos \left(\frac{\pi}{6} t - \frac{\pi}{4}\right)$$
$$y_2 = 12$$

Set your horizontal axis ($x$ or $t$) to a window like $[0, 10]$ to see the first few hours clearly. Ensure your calculator is in **Radians** mode, as the time variable $t$ is inside trigonometric functions with $\pi$.

gdc.instructions:
1. Go to the Graph menu.
2. Enter the power function as f1(x) and 12 as f2(x).
3. Ensure the angle mode is set to Radians.
4. Adjust the window to see the first intersection (e.g., x from 0 to 10).

ti84.instructions:
1. Press [Y=].
2. Enter Y1 = 10 + 8*sin((π/6)*X) + 6*e^(-0.01*X)*cos((π/6)*X - π/4).
3. Enter Y2 = 12.
4. Press [WINDOW] and set Xmin=0, Xmax=10.
5. Press [GRAPH].

2. In the second line, type: y = 12.
3. Click on the first intersection point where the curve crosses the line downwards.

calculator.description: Plotting the power function and the threshold line to find their intersection.

steps.title: Find the intersection point
steps.content:
Observe the graph. At $t=0$, $P(0) \approx 14.24$, so the generator starts in an operational state. As time increases, the curve eventually dips. We need the first point where it crosses $y = 12$ from above to below.

Using the **Intersect** tool on your GDC:

$$\text{Intersection at } t \approx 5.08$$

gdc.instructions:

2. Select 'Intersection'.
3. Identify the first point where the graph goes below the line y=12.

2. Select '5: intersect'.
3. Select the first curve (Y1), the second curve (Y2), and move the cursor near the first downward crossing for the 'Guess'.

steps.title: State final answer
steps.content:
The first time the generator is no longer operational ($P(t) < 12$) occurs at:

$$t \approx 5.08 \text{ hours}$$

This means that approximately 5.08 hours after midnight, the power output falls below the required 12 megawatts.

steps.title: Define the boundary condition
steps.content:
The question states that the generator is operational when $P(t) \geq 12$. To find the exact moment it stops being operational, we need to find the first time $t$ after midnight when the power output equals 12 MW and is decreasing.

We start by setting up the equation:
$$10+8 \sin \left(\frac{\pi}{6} t\right)+6 e^{-0.01 t} \cos \left(\frac{\pi}{6} t-\frac{\pi}{4}\right) = 12$$

highlights.text: the generator is considered operational when P(t) >= 12

steps.title: Evaluate the initial state
steps.content:
At $t = 0$ (midnight), the power output is:
$$P(0) = 10 + 8(0) + 6e^{0}\cos\left(-\frac{\pi}{4}\right) \approx 14.24 \text{ MW}$$

Since $14.24 > 12$, the generator starts the day in an operational state. As time increases, the power output will fluctuate. We are looking for the first time $t > 0$ where the power drops below 12.

steps.title: Solve using technology
steps.content:
Using the **Numerical Equation Solver** (or $nSolve$) on your GDC is the most efficient way to handle this complex equation. This corresponds to **Syllabus Topic SL 1.8**, which covers using technology to solve equations.

Input the equation into your solver. Since we know the power starts at 14.24 and we are looking for when it falls to 12, providing an initial guess (like $x = 5$) will help the calculator find the correct root quickly.

gdc.instructions:
1. Go to the Equation Solver menu.
2. Enter the equation: 10 + 8*sin((π/6)*t) + 6*e^(-0.01*t)*cos((π/6)*t - π/4) = 12
3. Set a lower bound of 0 and an initial guess of 5.
4. Solve for t.

ti84.instructions:
1. Press [MATH] and select 'B:Solver...'
2. Enter the equation: 0 = 10 + 8*sin((π/6)*X) + 6*e^(-0.01*X)*cos((π/6)*X - π/4) - 12
3. Provide an initial guess for X (e.g., X=5).
4. Press [ALPHA] [SOLVE].

desmos.instructions:
1. Type the function: f(x) = 10 + 8*sin((pi/6)*x) + 6*e^(-0.01*x)*cos((pi/6)*x - pi/4)
2. Type: y = 12
3. Look for the first intersection point where the graph of f(x) is going downward.

calculator.description: Solve the equation P(t) = 12 for the first crossing point where power is decreasing.

steps.title: Identify the final time
steps.content:
The numerical solver gives us the value $t \approx 5.0837...$ 

Rounding to three significant figures (as is standard in IB), we find the first time the generator is not operational is $t = 5.08$ hours after midnight.

parts.content: The revenue generated, $R(t)$, in thousands of dollars per hour, is modeled by $R(t)= 2 P(t)+0.01 P(t)^{2}$. Calculate the total revenue over the first 48 hours, correct to two decimal places, using numerical integration.

steps.title: Formulate the total revenue
steps.content:
Total revenue is the accumulation of the revenue rate $R(t)$ over a specific time interval. Since the rate $R(t)$ is given in thousands of dollars per hour, the total revenue over the first 48 hours is the definite integral of $R(t)$ from $t = 0$ to $t = 48$:

$$\text{Total Revenue} = \int_{0}^{48} R(t) \, dt$$

highlights.text: total revenue over the first 48 hours

steps.title: Define the power function
steps.content:
To calculate this efficiently and accurately, we use the graphing calculator (GDC). First, we define the power function $P(t)$ as our first variable. 

In the function editor (usually the $Y=$ screen), enter the equation for $P(t)$ provided in the question:

$$P(t)=10+8 \sin \left(\frac{\pi}{6} t\right)+6 e^{-0.01 t} \cos \left(\frac{\pi}{6} t-\frac{\pi}{4}\right)$$ 

**Note:** Ensure your calculator is in **Radian mode** for all trigonometric calculations.

Next, define the revenue function $R(t)$ using the $P(t)$ function you just entered. This saves time and reduces the risk of typing errors.

$$R(t)= 2 P(t)+0.01 P(t)^{2}$$

On your GDC, set this up as a second function that references the first one.

Now, use the numerical integration tool on the main calculation screen to find the total revenue. We integrate the revenue function $R(t)$ from $t=0$ to $t=48$:

$$\text{Total Revenue} = \int_{0}^{48} (2 P(t) + 0.01 P(t)^2) \, dt$$

$$\text{Total Revenue} = 1049.00$$ 

Don't forget the units: the revenue is in thousands of dollars.

**Total Revenue:** $1049.00$ thousand dollars.

steps.title: Set up the integral
steps.content:
To find the total revenue accumulated over a period of time, we need to integrate the revenue rate $R(t)$ with respect to time. Since we want the total over the first 48 hours, our lower limit is $0$ and our upper limit is $48$.

$$\text{Total Revenue} = \int_{0}^{48} R(t) \, dt$$

Substituting the expression for $R(t)$ provided in the question:

$$\text{Total Revenue} = \int_{0}^{48} (2P(t) + 0.01(P(t))^2) \, dt$$

3. Define $Y_2$ as the revenue function $R(t)$ using $Y_1$:
   $$Y_2 = 2 Y_1 + 0.01 (Y_1)^2$$

steps.title: Set up the integral
steps.content:
To find the total revenue over the first 48 hours, we need to integrate the revenue function $R(t)$ from $t = 0$ to $t = 48$. The expression for the integral is:

$$\text{Total Revenue} = \int_{0}^{48} R(t) \, dt$$

Substituting the expression for $R(t)$ in terms of $P(t)$:

$$\text{Total Revenue} = \int_{0}^{48} (2P(t) + 0.01P(t)^2) \, dt$$

where $$P(t) = 10+8 \sin \left(\frac{\pi}{6} t\right)+6 e^{-0.01 t} \cos \left(\frac{\pi}{6} t-\frac{\pi}{4}\right)$$.

highlights.text: total revenue over the first 48 hours

steps.title: Identify the Trapezoidal Rule
steps.content:
The **Trapezoidal Rule** allows us to approximate a definite integral by dividing the area under the curve into $n$ trapezoids of equal width $h$. According to the Data Booklet:

$$\int_{a}^{b} y \, dx \approx \frac{h}{2} \left( (y_0 + y_n) + 2(y_1 + y_2 + \dots + y_{n-1}) \right)$$

where:
- $a = 0$ and $b = 48$
- $h = \frac{b - a}{n}$ is the width of each sub-interval
- $y_i = R(t_i)$ is the value of the function at each step $t_i$

steps.title: Apply the rule with sub-intervals
steps.content:
To calculate this numerically, we divide the 48-hour period into sub-intervals. For example, if we use $n = 8$ sub-intervals, the width $h$ is:

$$h = \frac{48 - 0}{8} = 6 \text{ hours}$$

We then evaluate $R(t)$ at $t = 0, 6, 12, \dots, 48$. The formula becomes:

$$\text{Revenue} \approx \frac{6}{2} [ (R(0) + R(48)) + 2(R(6) + R(12) + R(18) + R(24) + R(30) + R(36) + R(42)) ]$$

As we increase the number of sub-intervals ($n \to \infty$), the approximation becomes more precise. In your exam, you should use your GDC's numerical integration function, which effectively applies this logic with high precision.

parts.content: Show that the maximum displacement occurs at $t=0$.
parts.markscheme:
Derivative:

$x^{\prime}(t)=-0.05 e^{-0.1 t} \cos \left(\frac{\pi}{2} t\right)-0.5 e^{-0.1 t} \cdot \frac{\pi}{2} \sin \left(\frac{\pi}{2} t\right)$.
<marks>M1</marks>

For $t\ge 0$, $e^{-0.1t}\le 1$ and $\cos\left(\frac{\pi}{2}t\right)\le 1$, so

$x(t)=0.5e^{-0.1t}\cos\left(\frac{\pi}{2}t\right)\le 0.5\cdot 1\cdot 1=0.5=x(0)$.

Equality occurs at $t=0$, hence the maximum displacement (for $t\ge 0$) occurs at $t=0$.

questions.specification: It is believed that two variables, $v$ and $w$, are related by the equation $v = k w^n$, where $k, n \in \mathbb{R}$. Experimental values of $v$ and $w$ are obtained. A graph of $\ln v$ against $\ln w$ shows a straight line passing through $(-1.7, 4.3)$ and $(7.1, 17.5)$.

labeledSubSubTopics.path: Functions > AHL 2.10—Scaling large numbers, log-log graphs > Question Type 4: Using log-log and semi-log variables for estimation of parameters
labeledSubSubTopics.title: Question Type 4: Using log-log and semi-log variables for estimation of parameters
labeledSubSubTopics.reasoning: The model v = k w^n becomes ln v = ln k + n ln w; using two points on a straight-line plot of ln v against ln w to find slope n and intercept ln k is parameter estimation from a log-log graph.

parts.content: Find the value of $k$ and of $n$.
parts.markscheme:
- **State** the linear relationship by taking natural logarithms:
  $$ \ln v = \ln(k w^n) = \ln k + n \ln w $$ <marks>M1</marks>
- Identify that this is a linear form $Y = mX + c$ where $Y = \ln v$, $X = \ln w$, gradient $m = n$, and intercept $c = \ln k$.
- **Calculate** the gradient $n$ using points $(-1.7, 4.3)$ and $(7.1, 17.5)$:
  $$ n = \frac{17.5 - 4.3}{7.1 - (-1.7)} = \frac{13.2}{8.8} = 1.5 $$ <marks>A1</marks>
- **Substitute** the point $(-1.7, 4.3)$ into the linear equation to find the intercept $\ln k$:
  $$ 4.3 = 1.5(-1.7) + \ln k $$ <marks>M1</marks>
- **Solve** for $\ln k$:
  $$ 4.3 = -2.55 + \ln k \implies \ln k = 6.85 $$
- **Calculate** the value of $k$:
  $$ k = e^{6.85} \approx 944 $$ <marks>A1</marks>

4 marks total

parts.markscheme: $C(0) = 0.1 + 0.05 \sin \left(\frac{\pi}{3}\right) + 0.03 e^{0} = 0.13 + 0.05 \left(\frac{\sqrt{3}}{2}\right) \approx 0.173 \text{ mol L}^{-1}$ <marks>A1</marks>

steps.title: Identify "initial" condition
steps.content:
In mathematical modeling, the word "initial" refers to the very start of the process. For a function dependent on time $t$, this always corresponds to $t = 0$. 

We need to find the value of $C(0)$.

steps.title: Substitute $t = 0$
steps.content:
Using the algebraic substitution method, we replace every instance of $t$ in the concentration function with $0$:

$$C(0) = 0.1 + 0.05 \sin \left( \frac{\pi}{4}(0) + \frac{\pi}{3} \right) + 0.03 e^{-0.1(0)}$$

steps.title: Simplify the expression
steps.content:
Now, let's simplify the terms inside the function:

1.  **Trigonometric term**: $\frac{\pi}{4}(0) + \frac{\pi}{3} = \frac{\pi}{3}$
2.  **Exponential term**: $-0.1(0) = 0$

This gives us:
$$C(0) = 0.1 + 0.05 \sin \left( \frac{\pi}{3} \right) + 0.03 e^{0}$$

Recall that any non-zero number raised to the power of $0$ is $1$, so $e^0 = 1$.

steps.title: Evaluate with technology
steps.content:
To get the final numerical value, we can evaluate the expression. Remember that $\sin\left(\frac{\pi}{3}\right) = \frac{\sqrt{3}}{2} \approx 0.866$. 

$$\begin{aligned} C(0) &= 0.1 + 0.05(0.866...) + 0.03(1) \\ C(0) &= 0.13 + 0.0433... \end{aligned}$$

$$C(0) \approx 0.173 \text{ mol L}^{-1}$$

At $t=2, C'(2) = 0.03927 \cos \left(\frac{5\pi}{6}\right) - 0.003 e^{-0.2} \approx -0.0365 \text{ mol L}^{-1} \text{ s}^{-1}$. <marks>A1</marks>

steps.title: Identify the goal
steps.content: The question asks for the "rate of change of concentration" at a specific time. In calculus, the rate of change of a function is given by its derivative. Therefore, we need to find the derivative $C'(t)$ and then evaluate it at $t=2$.

highlights.text: rate of change of concentration at t=2

steps.title: Differentiate the sine term
steps.content:
To differentiate the term $0.05 \sin \left(\frac{\pi}{4} t+\frac{\pi}{3}\right)$, we use the **chain rule**. 

The derivative of $\sin(u)$ is $\cos(u) \cdot u'$. Here, $u = \frac{\pi}{4} t+\frac{\pi}{3}$, so its derivative with respect to $t$ is $\frac{\pi}{4}$.

$$\frac{d}{dt} \left[ 0.05 \sin \left(\frac{\pi}{4} t+\frac{\pi}{3}\right) \right] = 0.05 \cdot \frac{\pi}{4} \cos \left(\frac{\pi}{4} t+\frac{\pi}{3}\right)$$

This is a core skill from **Topic 5: Calculus (AHL 5.9)** regarding derivative rules.

steps.title: Differentiate the exponential term
steps.content:
Next, we differentiate the term $0.03 e^{-0.1 t}$, again using the **chain rule**. 

The derivative of $e^{u}$ is $e^{u} \cdot u'$. Here, $u = -0.1t$, so its derivative is $-0.1$.

$$\frac{d}{dt} [0.03 e^{-0.1 t}] = 0.03 \cdot (-0.1) e^{-0.1 t} = -0.003 e^{-0.1 t}$$

steps.title: State the full derivative
steps.content:
Now we combine our results. Note that the derivative of the constant $0.1$ is $0$. The full derivative function $C'(t)$ is:

$$C'(t) = 0.05 \cdot \frac{\pi}{4} \cos \left(\frac{\pi}{4} t + \frac{\pi}{3}\right) - 0.003 e^{-0.1 t}$$

Finding this correct expression earns you the first two marks (**M1A1**).

steps.title: Substitute t = 2
steps.content:
To find the rate at $t=2$, we substitute $2$ into our derivative function:

$$C'(2) = 0.05 \cdot \frac{\pi}{4} \cos \left(\frac{\pi}{4} (2) + \frac{\pi}{3}\right) - 0.003 e^{-0.1 (2)}$$

Simplifying the angle inside the cosine:
$$\frac{\pi}{2} + \frac{\pi}{3} = \frac{3\pi + 2\pi}{6} = \frac{5\pi}{6}$$

steps.title: Calculate the final value
steps.content:
Now we evaluate the expression numerically using a calculator. 

$$C'(2) \approx -0.036464...$$

The question asks for **three significant figures**. Rounding our result, we get:

$$-0.0365 \text{ mol L}^{-1} \text{ s}^{-1}$$

steps.content:
The calculator yields a value of approximately $-0.0364649...$

Rounding to **three significant figures**, the rate of change is:
$$-0.0365 \text{ mol L}^{-1} \text{ s}^{-1}$$

This negative value indicates that the concentration is decreasing at $t=2$.

steps.title: Define the rate of change
steps.content: In mathematics and science, the "rate of change" of a function at a specific point is equivalent to the **gradient** (or slope) of the curve at that point. Since we are given the concentration function $C(t)$, we need to find the gradient of $C(t)$ when $t = 2$. Using a Graphing Display Calculator (GDC) allows us to do this quickly without manual differentiation.

$$y_1 = 0.1 + 0.05 \sin \left(\frac{\pi}{4} x + \frac{\pi}{3}\right) + 0.03 e^{-0.1 x}$$

steps.title: Analyze the gradient
steps.content:
By using the gradient tool ($dy/dx$) at $x = 2$, the calculator provides the numerical value of the slope. 

From the graph analysis:
$$\frac{dC}{dt} \text{ at } t=2 \approx -0.0364649...$$

steps.title: Final rounding
steps.content:
The question asks for the answer correct to **three significant figures**. 

Starting from the first non-zero digit:
1. '3' is the first sig fig.
2. '6' is the second.
3. '4' is the third, but we look at the next digit ('6') to round up.

Thus, the rate of change is approximately $-0.0365 \text{ mol L}^{-1} \text{ s}^{-1}$.

parts.content: Calculate the population of bacteria at $t = 3$ hours.
parts.markscheme:
* Substitute $t = 3$ into the population formula <marks>M1</marks>
* $P(3) = 1000 + 300 \ln(3 + 1)$
* $P(3) = 1415.88...$
* $P(3) = 1416$ (or $1420$) <marks>A1</marks>

[2 marks]

steps.title: Identify the given information
steps.content: To find the population at a specific time, we use the provided function $P(t) = 1000 + 300 \ln(t + 1)$. The question asks us to find the population when $t = 3$ hours.

highlights.text: Calculate the population of bacteria at t = 3 hours.

steps.title: Substitute the value
steps.content:
Using the **Direct Algebraic Substitution** method, we replace every instance of $t$ in the formula with the value $3$:

$$P(3) = 1000 + 300 \ln(3 + 1)$$

This simplifies to:

$$P(3) = 1000 + 300 \ln(4)$$

In the IB markscheme, showing this substitution step is essential to earn the first method mark (**M1**). This concept is covered in **Topic 2: Functions** under modelling skills.

steps.title: Evaluate using technology
steps.content:
Now, we use a calculator to find the numerical value. Note that $\ln$ refers to the natural logarithm (logarithm to the base $e$).

$$\begin{aligned} P(3) &= 1000 + 300 \times 1.38629\dots \\ P(3) &= 1000 + 415.888\dots \\ P(3) &= 1415.888\dots \end{aligned}$$

gdc.instructions: Enter the expression 1000 + 300 * ln(4) into the calculation menu and execute.

calculator.description: Calculate the population by substituting t=3 into the function.

steps.title: Final answer and rounding
steps.content:
The final value is $1415.888\dots$.

In IB exams, unless otherwise stated, you should round your answer to 3 significant figures. However, since we are dealing with a population of bacteria, rounding to the nearest whole number is also common practice.

$$P(3) \approx 1416 \text{ (nearest whole number)}$$

$$P(3) \approx 1420 \text{ (3 significant figures)}$$

Both versions are accepted for the accuracy mark (**A1**).

parts.content: Find the rate of change of the population at $t = 5$ hours.
parts.markscheme:
* Attempt to differentiate $P(t)$ <marks>M1</marks>
* $\frac{dP}{dt} = \frac{300}{t + 1}$
* Substitute $t = 5$ into the derivative
* $\frac{dP}{dt} = \frac{300}{5 + 1} = 50$ (bacteria per hour) <marks>A1</marks>

[2 marks]

steps.title: Identify the goal
steps.content: To find the **rate of change** of the population, we need to calculate the derivative of the population function, $P'(t)$. This is a common application of calculus where the derivative represents how fast the population is growing at a specific moment in time.

steps.title: Differentiate the function
steps.content:
We differentiate $P(t) = 1000 + 300 \ln(t + 1)$ with respect to $t$. 

1. The derivative of the constant $1000$ is $0$.
2. To differentiate $300 \ln(t+1)$, we use the rule $\frac{d}{dx}(\ln(x)) = \frac{1}{x}$ and the **chain rule**.

$$\begin{aligned} P'(t) &= 0 + 300 \cdot \frac{1}{t+1} \cdot \frac{d}{dt}(t+1) \\ P'(t) &= \frac{300}{t+1} \cdot 1 \\ P'(t) &= \frac{300}{t+1} \end{aligned}$$

Finding this derivative correctly earns you the first mark (M1). This topic is covered in **AHL 5.9—Differentiating standard functions and derivative rules**.

highlights.text: AHL 5.9—Differentiating standard functions and derivative rules

steps.title: Evaluate at t = 5
steps.content:
Now, we substitute the given time into our derivative to find the specific rate at that moment:

$$\begin{aligned} P'(5) &= \frac{300}{5 + 1} \\ P'(5) &= \frac{300}{6} \end{aligned}$$

steps.title: Verify with technology
steps.content: While we are solving this analytically, you can use your GDC to verify the numerical value of the derivative at a point.

gdc.instructions: Use the numerical derivative tool (usually found in the Math or Calculus menu). Enter the function and set the variable to 5.

calculator.description: Calculate the numerical derivative of P(t) at t = 5.

steps.title: State the final answer
steps.content:
Dividing $300$ by $6$ gives us the final rate:

$$P'(5) = 50$$

The population is increasing at a rate of **$50$ bacteria per hour**. This final result earns the accuracy mark (A1).

$$\left. \frac{dP}{dt} \right|_{t=5}$$

parts.content: Determine the time at which the bacteria population reaches 1500.
parts.markscheme:
* Set the population equation equal to 1500 <marks>M1</marks>
* $1500 = 1000 + 300 \ln(t + 1)$
* $500 = 300 \ln(t + 1) \Rightarrow \ln(t + 1) = \frac{5}{3}$
* $t = e^{\frac{5}{3}} - 1 = 4.29$ (hours) <marks>A1</marks>

[2 marks]

steps.title: Set up the equation
steps.content:
To find the time when the bacteria population reaches 1500, we need to set the function $P(t)$ equal to 1500.

$$1500 = 1000 + 300 \ln(t + 1)$$

This is a standard step in modelling questions: replacing the output variable with a known value to solve for the input variable.

steps.title: Isolate the log term
steps.content:
Our goal is to isolate the natural log term. First, subtract 1000 from both sides of the equation:

$$\begin{aligned} 1500 - 1000 &= 300 \ln(t + 1) \\ 500 &= 300 \ln(t + 1) \end{aligned}$$

steps.title: Divide to simplify
steps.content:
Next, divide both sides by 300 to get the logarithm by itself. 

$$\begin{aligned} \frac{500}{300} &= \ln(t + 1) \\ \frac{5}{3} &= \ln(t + 1) \end{aligned}$$

It is usually better to keep this as a fraction ($\frac{5}{3}$) rather than a decimal to maintain accuracy in your final answer.

steps.title: Convert to exponential form
steps.content:
To solve for $t$, we need to 'undo' the natural logarithm. Since the base of a natural log is $e$, we rewrite the equation in exponential form:

$$t + 1 = e^{\frac{5}{3}}$$

This step uses the fundamental definition of logarithms covered in **Topic 1.5: Exponents and Logarithms**.

steps.title: Solve for t
steps.content:
Finally, subtract 1 from both sides to isolate $t$:

$$t = e^{\frac{5}{3}} - 1$$

Now we can use a calculator to find the numerical value.

gdc.instructions: Use the exponential function e^x with x = 5/3, then subtract 1.

ti84.instructions: Press [2nd] [ln] to get e^, then type (5/3), arrow right, and subtract 1.

desmos.instructions: Type e^(5/3) - 1 into the expression bar.

calculator.description: Calculate the value of the exponential expression.

steps.title: State the final answer
steps.content:
Calculating the value gives:

$$t \approx 4.29 \text{ hours}$$

In IB exams, remember to round your final answer to **3 significant figures** unless the question states otherwise. Here, the first mark (M1) is for setting the equation to 1500 and the second (A1) is for the final correct value.

questions.specification:
A drone's altitude, $A(t)$, in meters, above a field at time $t$ seconds is modeled by

$$
A(t)=50+30 \sin \left(\frac{\pi}{20} t\right)+20 e^{-0.02 t} \cos \left(\frac{\pi}{20} t+\frac{\pi}{3}\right) .
$$

The drone's battery life depends on its altitude, with power consumption rate $B(t)= 0.1 A(t)$ watts.

parts.content: Find the period of the drone's altitude oscillations.
parts.markscheme:
Period: $\frac{2 \pi}{\frac{\pi}{20}}=40$ seconds.
<marks>M1</marks><marks>A1</marks>

parts.content: Express the oscillatory part as a single trigonometric function with time-dependent amplitude.
parts.markscheme:
Let $\theta=\frac{\pi}{20}t$.

$20e^{-0.02t}\cos\left(\theta+\frac{\pi}{3}\right)=20e^{-0.02t}\left(\cos\theta\cos\frac{\pi}{3}-\sin\theta\sin\frac{\pi}{3}\right)=10e^{-0.02t}\cos\theta-10\sqrt{3}\,e^{-0.02t}\sin\theta$.
<marks>M1</marks>

Oscillatory part:
$(30-10\sqrt{3}\,e^{-0.02t})\sin\theta+(10e^{-0.02t})\cos\theta$.

Write as $R(t)\sin(\theta+\phi(t))$ where
$R(t)=\sqrt{(30-10\sqrt{3}\,e^{-0.02t})^{2}+(10e^{-0.02t})^{2}}=\sqrt{900-600\sqrt{3}\,e^{-0.02t}+400e^{-0.04t}}$.
<marks>A1</marks>

$\phi(t)=\tan^{-1}\left(\frac{10e^{-0.02t}}{30-10\sqrt{3}\,e^{-0.02t}}\right)$.
<marks>A1</marks>

So the oscillatory part can be written as
$R(t)\sin\left(\frac{\pi}{20}t+\phi(t)\right)$.
<marks>A1</marks>
<marks>A1</marks>

parts.content: Find the total energy consumed by the drone over the first 40 seconds, correct to two decimal places.
parts.markscheme:
Energy $E=\int_{0}^{40} B(t)\,dt=\int_{0}^{40} 0.1A(t)\,dt$ (in watt-seconds, i.e. joules).
<marks>M1</marks>

$\int_{0}^{40}0.1\cdot 50\,dt=200$.

$\int_{0}^{40}0.1\cdot 30\sin\left(\frac{\pi}{20}t\right)dt=0$ (over one full period).
<marks>A1</marks>

For the exponential term,
$0.1\cdot 20\int_{0}^{40} e^{-0.02t}\cos\left(\frac{\pi}{20}t+\frac{\pi}{3}\right)dt\approx -5.53$.
<marks>A1</marks>

Total: $E\approx 200-5.53=194.47$ watt-seconds $=194.47$ J.
<marks>A1</marks>

parts.content: Determine the time when the drone first reaches an altitude of 60 meters.
parts.markscheme:
Solve $A(t)=60$.

At $t=0$:
$A(0)=50+30\sin 0+20e^0\cos\left(\frac{\pi}{3}\right)=50+0+20\cdot\frac12=60$.
<marks>M1</marks>

Therefore the first time is $t=0$ seconds.
<marks>A1</marks>
<marks>A1</marks>
<marks>A1</marks>

parts.content: Find the population of bacteria after 9 hours.
parts.markscheme:
- Substitute $t=9$ and $P_0=100$ into $P(t) = P_0 \times 2^{t/3}$ <marks>M1</marks>

- $P(9) = 100 \times 2^{9/3}$

- $P(9) = 100 \times 2^3$

- Calculate final population: $P(9) = 100 \times 8 = 800$ bacteria <marks>A1</marks>

2 marks total

<note>Award full marks for correct answer with clear working shown</note>

steps.title: Identify the growth parameters
steps.content:
To solve this using the exponential growth model, we first need to identify the key values from the problem. We are looking for the population $P(t)$ using the formula:

$$P(t) = P_0 \times 2^{t/k}$$

From the question, we have:
- The initial population, $P_0 = 100$
- The doubling period, $k = 3$ hours
- The time elapsed, $t = 9$ hours

steps.title: Substitute values into formula
steps.content:
Now, we substitute these specific values into our growth model. This step is crucial for earning your first mark in an IB exam.

$$P(9) = 100 \times 2^{9/3}$$

highlights.text: Substitute t=9 and P_0=100 into P(t) = P_0 × 2^{t/3}

steps.title: Simplify the exponent
steps.content:
Before calculating the final population, simplify the fraction in the exponent. The term $t/k$ represents how many times the population has doubled.

$$\frac{9}{3} = 3$$

So the population has doubled exactly $3$ times in the $9$-hour period. Our equation now looks like this:

$$P(9) = 100 \times 2^3$$

steps.title: Calculate the final population
steps.content:
Finally, we evaluate the power and multiply by the initial population:

$$\begin{aligned} P(9) &= 100 \times 8 \\ P(9) &= 800 \end{aligned}$$

There will be $800$ bacteria after $9$ hours.

steps.title: Identify the initial conditions
steps.content: To solve this using an iterative approach, we first note our starting point. We have an initial population of $100$ bacteria, and we are told the population doubles every $3$ hours.

steps.title: Determine the doubling intervals
steps.content:
Since we need to find the population after $9$ hours and it doubles every $3$ hours, we need to determine how many times this doubling occurs.

$$\text{Number of doublings} = \frac{9\text{ hours}}{3\text{ hours}} = 3$$

ti84.instructions: Enter 9 divided by 3.

steps.title: Iterate to 3 hours
steps.content:
Now we perform the first iteration. After the first $3$ hours, the initial population of $100$ doubles:

$$100 \times 2 = 200\text{ bacteria}$$

ti84.instructions: Enter 100 times 2.

calculator.description: Calculate population after first doubling.

steps.title: Iterate to 6 hours
steps.content:
For the second iteration, we take the population at $3$ hours ($200$) and double it again to find the population at $6$ hours:

$$200 \times 2 = 400\text{ bacteria}$$

ti84.instructions: Enter 200 times 2.

calculator.description: Calculate population after second doubling.

steps.title: Iterate to 9 hours
steps.content:
For the third and final iteration, we double the population found at $6$ hours to reach our target of $9$ hours:

$$400 \times 2 = 800\text{ bacteria}$$

ti84.instructions: Enter 400 times 2.

steps.title: Final answer and marking
steps.content:
The population after $9$ hours is $800$ bacteria.

In an IB exam, showing the steps of the doubling process (or substituting into the exponential formula) earns you the method mark **(M1)**, and the correct final value earns the accuracy mark **(A1)**.

steps.title: Define the sequence parameters
steps.content:
To solve this using a geometric sequence, we first identify the starting value and the growth factor. 

We are told the population starts at 100, so we can set the first term of our sequence, $u_1$, to 100. Because the population doubles, the common ratio $r$ is 2.

steps.title: Calculate doubling periods
steps.content:
Next, we need to find how many times the population doubles in 9 hours. Since it doubles every 3 hours, we calculate the number of doubling periods:

$$\text{Periods} = \frac{9}{3} = 3$$

In a geometric sequence $u_n = u_1 r^{n-1}$, the exponent $(n-1)$ represents the number of times the ratio has been applied. Here, the ratio has been applied 3 times ($n-1 = 3$), which means we are looking for the 4th term, $u_4$.

steps.title: Apply geometric sequence formula
steps.content:
Now we use the geometric sequence formula from the **Number and Algebra** section of your data booklet:

$$u_{n}=u_{1}r^{n-1}$$

Substituting our values ($u_1 = 100$, $r = 2$, and $n-1 = 3$):

$$u_4 = 100 \times 2^3$$

steps.title: Find final population
steps.content:
Finally, calculate the value of the expression:

$$\begin{aligned} u_4 &= 100 \times 8 \\ u_4 &= 800 \end{aligned}$$

The population of bacteria after 9 hours is 800.

- Take $\log_2$ of both sides: $\log_2(16) = \log_2(2^{t/3})$

- Simplify: $4 = t/3$ (since $16 = 2^4$)

- Solve for $t$: $t = 12$ hours <marks>A1</marks>

questions.specification:
A local bakery calculates its profit $P$ in dollars based on the number of dozens of pastries it produces, denoted as $x$, using the equation:

$$P = x\left(k - \frac{5}{200}x^2\right)$$

where $k$ is a constant representing the maximum potential profit per dozen.

labeledSubSubTopics.path: Calculus > SL 5.7—Optimisation > Question Type 2: Optimizing profit or cost functions under a given context
labeledSubSubTopics.title: Question Type 2: Optimizing profit or cost functions under a given context
labeledSubSubTopics.reasoning: Parts (b) and (d) require finding and interpreting the maximum profit of a contextual profit function using calculus (optimization in a real-world setting).

labeledSubSubTopics.title: Question Type 1: Finding the derivative of a polynomial with different ranges of integers
labeledSubSubTopics.reasoning: Part (a) asks for an explicit derivative dP/dx of a polynomial expression in x (with parameter k).

labeledSubSubTopics.title: Question Type 1: Finding the first order conditions for polynomials up to cubics
labeledSubSubTopics.reasoning: To maximize profit in (b)/(d), the method involves setting dP/dx = 0 (first-order condition) to find the stationary point.

parts.content: Find an expression for $\frac{dP}{dx}$ in terms of $k$ and $x$.
parts.markscheme:
### Method

Differentiating $P = kx - \frac{5}{200}x^3$ with respect to $x$:

$$\frac{dP}{dx} = k - \frac{15}{200}x^2$$

Simplifying the fraction:

$$\frac{dP}{dx} = k - \frac{3}{40}x^2$$

<marks>A1A1</marks>

2 marks total

<note>Award A1 for $k$ and A1 for $-\frac{3}{40}x^2$ (or equivalent).</note>

parts.content: Hence, calculate the maximum possible profit in terms of $k$, expressing your answer in the form $pk^{3/2}$, where $p$ is a constant.
parts.markscheme:
### Method 1

- Set the derivative equal to zero to find the critical point:
  $$k - \frac{3}{40}x^2 = 0 \quad \Rightarrow \quad x^2 = \frac{40k}{3}$$
  $$x = \sqrt{\frac{40k}{3}} = \frac{2\sqrt{10k}}{\sqrt{3}}$$ <marks>M1</marks>

- Substitute $x$ into the original profit equation:
  $$P = x\left(k - \frac{1}{40}x^2\right)$$
  $$P = \sqrt{\frac{40k}{3}}\left(k - \frac{1}{40}\left(\frac{40k}{3}\right)\right)$$
  $$P = \sqrt{\frac{40k}{3}}\left(k - \frac{k}{3}\right) = \sqrt{\frac{40k}{3}}\left(\frac{2k}{3}\right)$$ <marks>M1</marks>

- Simplify the expression:
  $$P = \frac{2\sqrt{10}\sqrt{k}}{\sqrt{3}} \cdot \frac{2k}{3} = \frac{4\sqrt{10}}{3\sqrt{3}}k^{3/2}$$
  Rationalizing the denominator:
  $$P = \frac{4\sqrt{30}}{9}k^{3/2}$$ <marks>A1</marks>

3 marks total

<note>Accept equivalent forms for $p$, e.g., $\frac{4\sqrt{10}}{3\sqrt{3}}$.</note>

steps.title: Expand the profit function
steps.content:
To prepare for differentiation using the power rule, we first expand the profit function. We distribute $x$ into the parentheses and simplify the fraction $\frac{5}{200}$ to $\frac{1}{40}$.

$$\begin{aligned} P &= x\left(k - \frac{5}{200}x^2\right) \\ P &= kx - \frac{1}{40}x^3 \end{aligned}$$

steps.title: Differentiate the function
steps.content:
We now find the derivative $P'$ with respect to $x$ using the **power rule**. 

$$\begin{aligned} P' &= \frac{d}{dx}(kx) - \frac{d}{dx}\left(\frac{1}{40}x^3\right) \\ P' &= k - \frac{3}{40}x^2 \end{aligned}$$

steps.title: Find the critical point
steps.content:
Maximum profit occurs when the gradient is zero ($P' = 0$). We solve for $x$ in terms of $k$:

$$\begin{aligned} k - \frac{3}{40}x^2 &= 0 \\ \frac{3}{40}x^2 &= k \\ x^2 &= \frac{40k}{3} \\ x &= \sqrt{\frac{40k}{3}} \end{aligned}$$

steps.title: Calculate maximum profit
steps.content:
Substitute our expression for $x$ (and $x^2$) back into the profit equation to find the maximum value:

$$\begin{aligned} P_{max} &= \sqrt{\frac{40k}{3}}\left(k - \frac{1}{40} \cdot \frac{40k}{3}\right) \\ P_{max} &= \sqrt{\frac{40k}{3}}\left(k - \frac{k}{3}\right) \\ P_{max} &= \sqrt{\frac{40k}{3}}\left(\frac{2k}{3}\right) \end{aligned}$$

steps.title: Simplify to required form
steps.content:
Finally, we simplify the expression into the form $pk^{3/2}$. 

$$\begin{aligned} P_{max} &= \frac{2k}{3} \cdot \frac{\sqrt{40}\sqrt{k}}{\sqrt{3}} \\ P_{max} &= \frac{2k}{3} \cdot \frac{2\sqrt{10}\sqrt{k}}{\sqrt{3}} \\ P_{max} &= \frac{4\sqrt{10}}{3\sqrt{3}} k^{3/2} \end{aligned}$$

Rationalizing the denominator by multiplying by $\frac{\sqrt{3}}{\sqrt{3}}$ gives:

$$P_{max} = \frac{4\sqrt{30}}{9} k^{3/2}$$

steps.title: Define terms for Product Rule
steps.content:
To find the maximum profit, we differentiate $P = x\left(k - \frac{5}{200}x^2\right)$. We treat this as a product of two functions, $u$ and $v$. First, let's simplify the constant term: $\frac{5}{200} = \frac{1}{40}$.

We define:
- $u = x$
- $v = k - \frac{1}{40}x^2$

steps.title: Differentiate u and v
steps.content:
Using the power rule from the data booklet, we find the derivatives of $u$ and $v$ with respect to $x$:

$$\begin{aligned} \frac{du}{dx} &= 1 \\ \frac{dv}{dx} &= -\frac{2}{40}x = -\frac{1}{20}x \end{aligned}$$

steps.title: Apply the Product Rule
steps.content:
Now we apply the product rule $\frac{dP}{dx} = u\frac{dv}{dx} + v\frac{du}{dx}$ and simplify the expression:

$$\begin{aligned} \frac{dP}{dx} &= x\left(-\frac{1}{20}x\right) + \left(k - \frac{1}{40}x^2\right)(1) \\ \frac{dP}{dx} &= -\frac{1}{20}x^2 + k - \frac{1}{40}x^2 \\ \frac{dP}{dx} &= k - \frac{3}{40}x^2 \end{aligned}$$

steps.title: Solve for stationary point
steps.content:
To find the maximum, we set the derivative to zero and solve for $x$:

$$\begin{aligned} k - \frac{3}{40}x^2 &= 0 \\ \frac{3}{40}x^2 &= k \\ x^2 &= \frac{40k}{3} \\ x &= \sqrt{\frac{40k}{3}} \end{aligned}$$

steps.title: Calculate maximum profit
steps.content:
Substitute $x = \sqrt{\frac{40k}{3}}$ back into the original profit function $P = x(k - \frac{1}{40}x^2)$:

$$\begin{aligned} P_{max} &= \sqrt{\frac{40k}{3}} \left( k - \frac{1}{40} \left( \frac{40k}{3} \right) \right) \\ P_{max} &= \sqrt{\frac{40k}{3}} \left( k - \frac{k}{3} \right) \\ P_{max} &= \sqrt{\frac{40k}{3}} \left( \frac{2k}{3} \right) \end{aligned}$$

steps.title: Final simplification
steps.content:
Finally, simplify the expression to the required form $pk^{3/2}$. We split the square root and rationalise:

$$\begin{aligned} P_{max} &= \frac{\sqrt{40}\sqrt{k}}{\sqrt{3}} \cdot \frac{2k}{3} \\ P_{max} &= \frac{2\sqrt{10} \cdot 2}{3\sqrt{3}} k^{3/2} \\ P_{max} &= \frac{4\sqrt{10}}{3\sqrt{3}} k^{3/2} \\ P_{max} &= \frac{4\sqrt{30}}{9} k^{3/2} \end{aligned}$$

gdc.instructions: Enter (4*sqrt(30))/9.

ti84.instructions: Type 4 * √(30) / 9 on the home screen.

desmos.instructions: Type 4 * sqrt(30) / 9.

parts.content: If the bakery's profit is $360$ when it produces 10 dozen pastries, determine the value of $k$.
parts.markscheme:
### Method 1

- The profit function is given by $P = x(k - \frac{5}{200}x^2)$.

- Substitute $x = 10$ and $P = 360$ into the equation:
  $$360 = 10\left(k - \frac{5}{200}(10)^2\right)$$ <marks>M1</marks>

- Solve for $k$:
  $$360 = 10\left(k - \frac{5}{200}(100)\right)$$
  $$360 = 10(k - 2.5)$$
  $$36 = k - 2.5$$
  $$k = 38.5$$ <marks>A1</marks>

- Therefore, $k = 38.5$.

2 marks total

parts.content:
A bakery's daily profit model is given by $P = x(k - \frac{5}{200}x^2)$
- $x$ is the number of dozens of pastries produced
- $P(x)$ is the profit in dollars

Find the optimal number of dozens of pastries the bakery should produce each morning to maximize its profit, giving your answer to the nearest whole number.
parts.markscheme:
### Method 1

- Find the critical point where $\frac{dP}{dx} = 0$:
  $$k - \frac{3}{40}x^2 = 0$$
  $$x = \sqrt{\frac{40k}{3}}$$ <marks>M1</marks>

- Substitute $k = 38.5$:
  $$x = \sqrt{\frac{40(38.5)}{3}} = \sqrt{\frac{1540}{3}}$$
  $$x \approx \sqrt{513.33} \approx 22.66$$ <marks>A1</marks>

- Round to the nearest whole number:
  $x = 23$ dozens

2 marks total

steps.title: Expand the profit function
steps.content:
To find the maximum profit using calculus, it is much easier to work with a polynomial. Let's expand the expression for $P$ by distributing the $x$:

$$\begin{aligned} P &= x\left(k - \frac{5}{200}x^2\right) \\ P &= kx - \frac{5}{200}x^3 \end{aligned}$$

We can simplify the fraction $\frac{5}{200}$ to $\frac{1}{40}$ to make our calculations cleaner:

$$P = kx - \frac{1}{40}x^3$$

steps.title: Find the first derivative
steps.content:
To find the maximum profit, we need to find the derivative $\frac{dP}{dx}$. We use the power rule from your data booklet:

$$\text{If } f(x) = x^n, \text{ then } f'(x) = nx^{n-1}$$

Applying this to our function:

$$\begin{aligned} \frac{dP}{dx} &= k - 3 \cdot \frac{1}{40}x^2 \\ \frac{dP}{dx} &= k - \frac{3}{40}x^2 \end{aligned}$$

steps.title: Set derivative to zero
steps.content:
A maximum profit occurs at a stationary point, where the gradient of the function is zero ($\frac{dP}{dx} = 0$). 

$$0 = k - \frac{3}{40}x^2$$

Now, we rearrange the equation to solve for $x$:

$$\begin{aligned} \frac{3}{40}x^2 &= k \\ x^2 &= \frac{40k}{3} \\ x &= \sqrt{\frac{40k}{3}} \end{aligned}$$

steps.title: Substitute and solve
steps.content:
Now we substitute the value of $k = 38.5$ into our formula to find the optimal value of $x$:

$$\begin{aligned} x &= \sqrt{\frac{40 \times 38.5}{3}} \\ x &= \sqrt{\frac{1540}{3}} \end{aligned}$$

Using a calculator, we find:

$$x \approx 22.656...$$

gdc.instructions: Enter the square root function and type 1540 / 3.

ti84.instructions: Press [2nd] [x²] (for √), then enter 1540 / 3 and press [ENTER].

desmos.instructions: Type sqrt(1540/3) in the input bar.

calculator.description: Calculate the square root to find the value of x.

steps.title: State final answer
steps.content:
The question asks for the answer to the **nearest whole number**. 

Since $22.656...$ rounds up, the optimal number of dozens of pastries is:

$$x = 23 \text{ dozens}$$

steps.title: Define the profit function
steps.content:
To find the optimal production level, we first need the full profit equation. Based on the previous parts of the question, we use the value $k = 38.5$. Substituting this into our profit model gives:

$$P(x) = x\left(38.5 - \frac{5}{200}x^2\right)$$

Simplifying the fraction $\frac{5}{200}$ to $0.025$, the function we will graph is:

$$P(x) = x(38.5 - 0.025x^2)$$ or $$P(x) = 38.5x - 0.025x^3$$

questions.specification:
A wind turbine's power output, $P(t)$, in kilowatts, varies over time $t$ hours according to the function

$$
P(t)=500+300 \sin \left(\frac{\pi}{12} t+\frac{\pi}{6}\right)+200 e^{-0.05 t} \cos \left(\frac{\pi}{12} t\right)
$$

where $t \geq 0$ and angles are measured in radians. The turbine operates efficiently when $P(t) \geq 600$. The wind conditions (and hence the oscillatory components of $P$) cycle every 24 hours.

parts.content: Express the oscillatory part of $P(t), 300 \sin \left(\frac{\pi}{12} t+\frac{\pi}{6}\right)+200 e^{-0.05 t} \cos \left(\frac{\pi}{12} t\right)$, in the form $A(t) \sin \left(\frac{\pi}{12} t+\phi(t)\right)$, where $A(t)$ and $\phi(t)$ are functions of $t$.
parts.markscheme:
Let $\theta=\frac{\pi}{12}t$ and $f(t)=300\sin\left(\theta+\frac{\pi}{6}\right)+200e^{-0.05t}\cos\theta$.

$300\sin\left(\theta+\frac{\pi}{6}\right)=300\left(\sin\theta\cos\frac{\pi}{6}+\cos\theta\sin\frac{\pi}{6}\right)=(150\sqrt{3})\sin\theta+150\cos\theta$.
<marks>M1</marks>

So $f(t)=(150\sqrt{3})\sin\theta+\left(150+200e^{-0.05t}\right)\cos\theta$.

Write $f(t)=A(t)\sin(\theta+\phi(t))=A(t)\cos\phi(t)\,\sin\theta+A(t)\sin\phi(t)\,\cos\theta$.
<marks>M1</marks>

Hence $A(t)\cos\phi(t)=150\sqrt{3}$ and $A(t)\sin\phi(t)=150+200e^{-0.05t}$.

$A(t)=\sqrt{(150\sqrt{3})^2+\left(150+200e^{-0.05t}\right)^2}=\sqrt{90000+60000e^{-0.05t}+40000e^{-0.1t}}$.
<marks>A1</marks>

$\phi(t)=\tan^{-1}\left(\frac{150+200e^{-0.05t}}{150\sqrt{3}}\right)$ (with appropriate quadrant if required).
<marks>A1</marks>

Accept unsimplified equivalent forms for $A(t)$ and $\phi(t)$.
<marks>A1</marks>

questions.specification:
A pendulum in a clock tower swings with a motion modeled by the angular displacement, $\theta(t)$, in radians, from its vertical position at time $t$ seconds, given by

$$
\theta(t)=0.2 e^{-0.02 t} \sin \left(\frac{\pi}{4} t+\frac{\pi}{3}\right)+0.1 \cos \left(\frac{\pi}{4} t\right)
$$

for $0 \leq t \leq 20$. The pendulum is considered to be in a "safe" range when $|\theta(t)| \leq 0.15$ radians.

parts.content: Find the period of the oscillatory components of $\theta(t)$.
parts.markscheme:
- Identify the angular frequency $\omega = \frac{\pi}{4}$ <marks>M1</marks>

- Calculate the period: $T = \frac{2\pi}{\pi/4} = 8$ seconds <marks>A1</marks>

2 marks total

steps.title: Identify oscillatory components
steps.content:
To find the period of the motion, we first look at the trigonometric parts of the function $\theta(t)$. The equation consists of a sine term and a cosine term:

$$\theta(t)=0.2 e^{-0.02 t} \sin \left(\frac{\pi}{4} t+\frac{\pi}{3}\right)+0.1 \cos \left(\frac{\pi}{4} t\right)$$

Both terms represent the oscillations of the pendulum. Notice that the values inside the trigonometric functions determine how fast the pendulum swings back and forth.

steps.title: Identify the angular frequency
steps.content:
In a general sinusoidal model of the form $y = a \sin(Bt + C)$ or $y = a \cos(Bt)$, the value $B$ represents the **angular frequency** (often denoted as $\omega$). This value is the coefficient of the variable $t$.

Looking at both trigonometric components in our specific function:
- For the sine term: $\sin \left(\frac{\pi}{4} t+\frac{\pi}{3}\right)$
- For the cosine term: $\cos \left(\frac{\pi}{4} t\right)$

We can see that the coefficient of $t$ is the same for both components:

$$\omega = B = \frac{\pi}{4}$$

steps.title: Calculate the period
steps.content:
The period ($T$) is the time it takes for the pendulum to complete one full cycle. We use the fundamental relationship between the period and the angular frequency:

$$T = \frac{2\pi}{B}$$

Substitute $B = \frac{\pi}{4}$ into the formula:

$$T = \frac{2\pi}{\frac{\pi}{4}}$$

To simplify this, we multiply by the reciprocal:

$$T = 2\pi \times \frac{4}{\pi} = 8$$

The period of the oscillatory components is **8 seconds**.

gdc.instructions: Calculate 2π divided by (π/4).

ti84.instructions: Enter (2 * π) / (π / 4) on the home screen.

desmos.instructions: Type the fraction expression to find the value of T.

calculator.description: Verify the calculation for the period T.

steps.title: Plot the function
steps.content:
To find the period using the graphical method, start by entering the displacement function $\theta(t)$ into your graphing calculator. 

$$\theta(t)=0.2 e^{-0.02 t} \sin \left(\frac{\pi}{4} t+\frac{\pi}{3}\right)+0.1 \cos \left(\frac{\pi}{4} t\right)$$

Set your graphing window for $0 \leq t \leq 20$ (the horizontal $x$-axis) and a vertical $y$-axis range of roughly $-0.5$ to $0.5$. Ensure your calculator is set to **Radians** mode, as the expression involves $\pi$.

ti84.instructions: Press [Y=] and enter the equation in Y1. Use [X] for [t]. Press [WINDOW] and set Xmin=0, Xmax=20. Press [GRAPH].

desmos.instructions: Type the equation into the expression bar. Use the wrench icon to set the x-axis from 0 to 20.

calculator.description: Enter the function and set the window.

steps.title: Find consecutive peaks
steps.content:
The period of an oscillation is the time it takes for one full cycle. Graphically, this is the horizontal distance between two consecutive peaks (maxima).

Use your calculator's **Maximum** tool to find the $t$-coordinates of the first two peaks on the graph.

gdc.instructions: Use the G-Solv (Graph Solve) tool and select 'MAX'. Use the arrow keys to toggle between the consecutive maximum points.

ti84.instructions: Press [2nd] [CALC] and select '4: maximum'. Move the cursor to the left and right of the first peak for the bounds, then hit [ENTER]. Repeat for the second peak.

desmos.instructions: Click on the curve and then click the grey dots appearing at the peaks to see their coordinates.

calculator.description: Find the x-values of the first and second peaks.

steps.title: Calculate the period
steps.content:
From the graph, you will find the first peak at approximately $t_1 \approx 0.61$ and the second peak at approximately $t_2 \approx 8.61$.

The period $T$ is the difference between these times:

$$T = t_2 - t_1$$
$$T = 8.61 - 0.61 = 8 \text{ seconds}$$

Even though the amplitude decreases slightly over time due to the $e^{-0.02t}$ term, the horizontal distance between the peaks remains constant.

steps.title: Verify with angular frequency
steps.content:
We can verify this result by looking at the angular frequency, $\omega$, which is the coefficient of $t$ in the trigonometric terms. In both parts of the function, $\omega = \frac{\pi}{4}$.

The relationship between the period and angular frequency is:

$$T = \frac{2\pi}{\omega}$$

By substituting our value:

$$T = \frac{2\pi}{\pi/4} = 8 \text{ seconds}$$

parts.content: Express $\theta(t)$ in the form $\theta(t)=A(t) \sin \left(\frac{\pi}{4} t+\phi(t)\right)$, where $A(t)$ and $\phi(t)$ are functions of $t$.
parts.markscheme:
- Expand $\sin(\frac{\pi}{4} t + \frac{\pi}{3})$: $\sin \frac{\pi}{4} t \cos \frac{\pi}{3} + \cos \frac{\pi}{4} t \sin \frac{\pi}{3}$ <marks>M1</marks>

- Group terms: $\theta(t) = (0.2 e^{-0.02 t} \cos \frac{\pi}{3}) \sin \frac{\pi}{4} t + (0.2 e^{-0.02 t} \sin \frac{\pi}{3} + 0.1) \cos \frac{\pi}{4} t$
- Identify coefficients: $a(t) = 0.1 e^{-0.02 t}$ and $b(t) = 0.1\sqrt{3} e^{-0.02 t} + 0.1$

- State expression for $A(t)$: $A(t)=\sqrt{(0.1 e^{-0.02 t})^2 + (0.1\sqrt{3} e^{-0.02 t} + 0.1)^2}$ <marks>A1</marks>

- State expression for $\phi(t)$: $\phi(t) = \tan^{-1}\left(\frac{0.1\sqrt{3} e^{-0.02 t} + 0.1}{0.1 e^{-0.02 t}}\right)$ <marks>A1</marks>

- Simplify $A(t)$: $A(t) = 0.1 \sqrt{4 e^{-0.04 t} + 2\sqrt{3} e^{-0.02 t} + 1}$ <marks>A1</marks>

- Simplify $\phi(t)$: $\phi(t) = \tan^{-1}(\sqrt{3} + e^{0.02t})$ <marks>A1</marks>

5 marks total

steps.title: Define axes and labels
steps.content:
To sketch the graph accurately, first define the axes based on the variables in the model. 

- The horizontal axis (x-axis) represents time, so label it $t$ (seconds).
- The vertical axis (y-axis) represents the angular displacement, so label it $\theta(t)$ (radians).

Since we are modeling the motion for $0 \leq t \leq 20$, the $t$-axis should span at least this range.

steps.content:
To find the points where the pendulum enters or leaves the "safe" range, we need to solve $|\theta(t)| = 0.15$. This means finding where the graph of $\theta(t)$ intersects the lines $y = 0.15$ and $y = -0.15$.

Enter the following into your graphing calculator:
- $Y_1 = 0.2 e^{-0.02 x} \sin \left(\frac{\pi}{4} x + \frac{\pi}{3}\right) + 0.1 \cos \left(\frac{\pi}{4} x\right)$
- $Y_2 = 0.15$
- $Y_3 = -0.15$

steps.content:
Using the 'intersect' tool on your GDC, identify the $t$-values where the displacement curve $Y_1$ meets the boundary lines $Y_2$ and $Y_3$. These represent the limits of the safe range.

There are 10 such points in the interval $[0, 20]$:
$$t \approx 1.7, 3.2, 5.7, 7.2, 9.6, 11.2, 13.6, 15.3, 17.5, 19.3$$

Mark these points on your sketch where the curve crosses the horizontal lines $y = \pm 0.15$.

steps.title: Describe the general shape
steps.content:
The graph is a decaying oscillation. To describe this shape for your sketch:

1.  **Oscillation**: Since the angular frequency of both terms is $\omega = \frac{\pi}{4}$, the period is:
    $$T = \frac{2\pi}{\omega} = \frac{2\pi}{\pi/4} = 8 \text{ seconds}$$
2.  **Decay**: The term $e^{-0.02t}$ acts as a damping factor. This means the peaks and troughs of the waves will get slightly smaller as time $t$ increases.

When sketching, ensure the curve starts at $\theta(0) \approx 0.273$ and gradually decreases in amplitude while completing $2.5$ cycles over $20$ seconds.

steps.title: Summary of sketch requirements
steps.content:
To earn full marks, your description or sketch must show:
- Correct labels: $t$ (s) and $\theta(t)$ (rad).
- Intersection points: Approximately $1.7, 3.2, 5.7, 7.2, 9.6, 11.2, 13.6, 15.3, 17.5, 19.3$ at the heights $y = \pm 0.15$.
- Periodic behavior: A repeating pattern every 8 seconds.
- Damping: The maximum height of each wave getting lower over time.

steps.title: Define the Axes
steps.content:
To begin our sketch, we need to establish the coordinate system based on the variables provided in the model.

- The horizontal axis (x-axis) represents **time**, denoted as $t$, and should be measured in **seconds**.
- The vertical axis (y-axis) represents the **angular displacement**, denoted as $\theta(t)$, and should be measured in **radians**.

According to the question, our graph should span the domain $0 \leq t \leq 20$.

highlights.text: x-axis as t (seconds), y-axis as θ(t) (radians)

steps.title: Analyze Period and Decay
steps.content:
Next, we examine the mathematical properties of the function $\theta(t)$ to understand its general shape.

1. **Period ($T$):** Both trigonometric terms involve the frequency coefficient $B = \frac{\pi}{4}$. The period of a sinusoidal function is given by:
   $$T = \frac{2\pi}{B}$$
   Substituting our value:
   $$T = \frac{2\pi}{\pi/4} = 8$$
   This means the pendulum completes one full oscillation every 8 seconds.

2. **Exponential Decay:** The term $e^{-0.02t}$ acts as a damping factor. Since the exponent is negative, the amplitude of the oscillations will gradually decrease over time as $t$ increases.

highlights.text: Oscillating function with period 8 and decaying amplitude

To finalize your description for the 3 marks, ensure you mention these three components:

$$[0, 1.73], [3.16, 5.68], [7.19, 9.62], [11.2, 13.6], [15.3, 17.5], [19.3, 20]$$

Note that we use square brackets because at the exact boundary values, the pendulum reaches the limit of the safe range.

steps.title: Define the mathematical condition
steps.content:
The question asks us to find when the pendulum is **outside** the safe range. We are given that the safe range is $|\theta(t)| \leq 0.15$ radians.

Therefore, the pendulum is outside the safe range when:

$$|\theta(t)| > 0.15$$

This absolute value inequality is equivalent to solving for two separate conditions:
1.  $\theta(t) > 0.15$ (swung too far to the right)
2.  $\theta(t) < -0.15$ (swung too far to the left)

steps.title: Graph the displacement function

Any part of the curve $\theta(t)$ that lies **above** $y = 0.15$ or **below** $y = -0.15$ represents a time when the pendulum is outside the safe range.

parts.content: The energy of the pendulum is modeled by $E(t)=k \theta(t)^{2}$, where $k$ is a constant. Given that the initial energy is $E(0)=0.5$ joules, find $k$ and the rate of change of energy at $t=5$ seconds, correct to three significant figures.
parts.markscheme:
- Calculate $\theta(0)$: $\theta(0) = 0.2 \sin(\frac{\pi}{3}) + 0.1 \cos(0) = 0.1\sqrt{3} + 0.1 \approx 0.273$ <marks>M1</marks>

- Solve for $k$: $k(0.2732)^2 = 0.5 \implies k \approx 6.70$ <marks>A1</marks>

- Use the chain rule for $E'(t)$: $E'(t) = 2k \theta(t) \theta'(t)$ <marks>A1</marks>

- Find $\theta(5)$ and $\theta'(5)$:
  $\theta(5) \approx -0.246$
  $\theta'(5) \approx 0.0958$ <marks>A1</marks>

- Calculate $E'(5)$: $2(6.70)(-0.246)(0.0958) \approx -0.315$ joules/second <marks>A1</marks>

5 marks total

steps.title: Find the initial displacement
steps.content:
To find $k$, we first need to determine the angular displacement at time $t = 0$. We substitute $t = 0$ into the given function for $\theta(t)$:

$$\theta(0) = 0.2 e^{-0.02(0)} \sin \left(\frac{\pi}{4}(0) + \frac{\pi}{3}\right) + 0.1 \cos \left(\frac{\pi}{4}(0)\right)$$

Since $e^0 = 1$ and $\cos(0) = 1$, this simplifies to:

$$\theta(0) = 0.2 \sin\left(\frac{\pi}{3}\right) + 0.1$$

Using the exact value $\sin\left(\frac{\pi}{3}\right) = \frac{\sqrt{3}}{2}$:

$$\theta(0) = 0.2\left(\frac{\sqrt{3}}{2}\right) + 0.1 = 0.1\sqrt{3} + 0.1 \approx 0.273205...$$

steps.title: Calculate the constant k
steps.content:
We are given that the initial energy $E(0) = 0.5$ Joules. Using the energy model $E(t) = k \theta(t)^2$, we can set up an equation to solve for $k$:

$$0.5 = k \cdot (0.273205...)^2$$

$$k = \frac{0.5}{(0.273205...)^2}$$

Using a calculator to find $k$ to three significant figures:

gdc.instructions: In the Run-Matrix menu, enter 0.5 ÷ (0.1 × √3 + 0.1)².

ti84.instructions:
1. Store 0.1*√(3)+0.1 as A. 
2. Calculate 0.5 / A².

calculator.description: Calculate the value of k using the initial energy and displacement squared.

steps.title: Apply the Chain Rule
steps.content:
To find the rate of change of energy, we need to find the derivative $E'(t)$. 

Since $E(t) = k [\theta(t)]^2$, we use the **Power Rule** and the **Chain Rule**. In the IB data booklet, the power rule is given as:

$$f(x)=x^{n} \Rightarrow f'(x)=nx^{n-1}$$

Applying the chain rule (multiplying by the derivative of the 'inside' function $\theta(t)$):

$$E'(t) = k \cdot 2[\theta(t)]^{2-1} \cdot \theta'(t)$$
$$E'(t) = 2k \theta(t) \theta'(t)$$

This expression allows us to calculate the rate of change at any time $t$ if we know the displacement and the velocity (rate of change of displacement) at that moment.

steps.title: Evaluate theta at t=5
steps.content:
Now we need the values of $\theta(5)$ and $\theta'(5)$. 

Substituting $t=5$ into the original displacement formula:
$$\theta(5) = 0.2 e^{-0.1} \sin \left(\frac{5\pi}{4} + \frac{\pi}{3}\right) + 0.1 \cos \left(\frac{5\pi}{4}\right)$$

Then, we use the calculator's numerical derivative function to find $\theta'(5)$ directly from the displacement equation. Let's use the calculator for both values.

steps.title: Final rate of change
steps.content:
From our previous steps, we have the following values:
- $k \approx 6.7023...$
- $\theta(5) \approx -0.2455...$
- $\theta'(5) \approx 0.09584...$

Now, substitute these into our chain rule formula for $E'(5)$:

$$E'(5) = 2 \cdot (6.7023...) \cdot (-0.2455...) \cdot (0.09584...)$$

Calculating this value:

$$E'(5) \approx -0.31536...$$

To **three significant figures**, we get:
$k = 6.70$ and $E'(5) = -0.315$ Joules/second.

steps.title: Calculate initial angular displacement
steps.content:
To find the constant $k$, we first need to determine the value of the angular displacement at time $t = 0$. We substitute $t = 0$ into the given model for $\theta(t)$:

$$\theta(0) = 0.2 e^{-0.02(0)} \sin \left(\frac{\pi}{4}(0) + \frac{\pi}{3}\right) + 0.1 \cos \left(\frac{\pi}{4}(0)\right)$$

Since $e^0 = 1$ and $\cos(0) = 1$, this simplifies to:

$$\theta(0) = 0.2 \sin\left(\frac{\pi}{3}\right) + 0.1$$

Using the exact value $\sin(\frac{\pi}{3}) = \frac{\sqrt{3}}{2}$:

$$\theta(0) = 0.2 \left(\frac{\sqrt{3}}{2}\right) + 0.1 = 0.1\sqrt{3} + 0.1 \approx 0.273205... \text{ radians}$$

steps.title: Solve for constant k
steps.content:
The question states that the energy model is $E(t) = k \theta(t)^2$ and the initial energy is $E(0) = 0.5$ joules. We use our value for $\theta(0)$ to solve for $k$:

$$\begin{aligned} E(0) &= k \cdot (\theta(0))^2 \\ 0.5 &= k \cdot (0.273205...)^2 \\ k &= \frac{0.5}{(0.273205...)^2} \\ k &\approx 6.6987... \end{aligned}$$

Rounding to three significant figures, we get **$k = 6.70$**.

steps.title: Establish the risk condition
steps.content:
The bridge is at risk when the oscillation amplitude, $A(t)$, exceeds $15\text{ cm}$. To find the first time this happens, we need to solve the equation where the amplitude is exactly $15$:

$$10 e^{-0.01 t} + 8 \sin \left(\frac{\pi}{30} t\right) - 6 \cos \left(\frac{\pi}{30} t\right) = 15$$

highlights.text: The bridge is at risk when A(t)>15.

steps.title: Simplify using Harmonic Identity
steps.content:
We can simplify the trigonometric part of the expression using the identity $a \sin \theta - b \cos \theta = R \sin(\theta - \alpha)$. 

Here, the trigonometric part is:
$$8 \sin \left(\frac{\pi}{30} t\right) - 6 \cos \left(\frac{\pi}{30} t\right)$$

where $a = 8$, $b = 6$, and $\theta = \frac{\pi}{30} t$.

steps.title: Calculate R and alpha
steps.content:
First, calculate the resultant amplitude $R$:
$$R = \sqrt{8^2 + 6^2} = \sqrt{100} = 10$$

Next, calculate the phase shift $\alpha$ in radians:
$$\alpha = \arctan\left(\frac{6}{8}\right) \approx 0.6435$$

So, the trigonometric part becomes:
$$10 \sin \left(\frac{\pi}{30} t - 0.6435\right)$$ 

Our full equation is now:
$$10 e^{-0.01 t} + 10 \sin \left(\frac{\pi}{30} t - 0.6435\right) = 15$$

parts.content: The energy stored in the oscillation is $E(t) = k A(t)^2$. Given $E(0) = 200$ joules, find the value of $k$ and the rate of energy change at $t = 30$, correct to three significant figures.
parts.markscheme:
$E(0) = k(4)^2 = 200 \Rightarrow 16k = 200$ <marks>M1</marks>

$k = 12.5$ <marks>A1</marks>

$E'(t) = 2 k A(t) A'(t)$ <marks>M1</marks>

$A'(t) = -0.1 e^{-0.01 t} + \frac{\pi}{30} \left[ 8 \cos \left(\frac{\pi}{30} t\right) + 6 \sin \left(\frac{\pi}{30} t\right) \right]$

At $t = 30$:

$A(30) = 10 e^{-0.3} + 8 \sin(\pi) - 6 \cos(\pi) \approx 13.408$ 

$A'(30) = -0.1 e^{-0.3} + \frac{\pi}{30} [-8 + 0] \approx -0.9118$ <marks>A1</marks>

$E'(30) = 2(12.5)(13.408)(-0.9118) \approx -306 \text{ joules/second}$ <marks>A1</marks>

steps.title: Determine the value of k
steps.content:
To find $k$, we use the initial condition $E(0) = 200$. First, we calculate the amplitude at $t=0$:

$$\begin{aligned} A(0) &= 10 e^{-0.01(0)} + 8 \sin(0) - 6 \cos(0) \\ &= 10(1) + 0 - 6(1) \\ &= 4 \text{ cm} \end{aligned}$$

Now, substitute this into the energy equation $E(t) = k A(t)^2$:

$$\begin{aligned} 200 &= k (4)^2 \\ 200 &= 16k \\ k &= 12.5 \end{aligned}$$

steps.title: Differentiate the amplitude function
steps.content:
We need the rate of change of energy, which involves the derivative of $A(t)$. Using the power rule and trigonometric derivative rules from the IB syllabus:

$$A'(t) = \frac{d}{dt} \left[ 10 e^{-0.01 t} + 8 \sin \left(\frac{\pi}{30} t\right) - 6 \cos \left(\frac{\pi}{30} t\right) \right]$$

Applying the chain rule to each term:

$$A'(t) = -0.1 e^{-0.01 t} + \frac{8\pi}{30} \cos \left(\frac{\pi}{30} t\right) + \frac{6\pi}{30} \sin \left(\frac{\pi}{30} t\right)$$ 

This uses the derivative rule for $x^n$ found in the data booklet, applied to the exponential and trigonometric functions.

steps.title: Apply the chain rule
steps.content:
The energy function is a composite function of the form $E(t) = k[A(t)]^2$. To find $E'(t)$, we apply the chain rule:

$$E'(t) = 2 \cdot k \cdot A(t) \cdot A'(t)$$

Substituting our known $k = 12.5$:

$$E'(t) = 25 \cdot A(t) \cdot A'(t)$$ 

This follows the rule for differentiating composite functions (Chain Rule), which is a key part of **Topic 5: Calculus**.

steps.content:
The value of $k$ is $12.5$. 

The rate of change of energy at $t=30$ is **$-306$ joules per second** (to 3 significant figures).

**Marks breakdown:**
- **M1**: Correct substitution for $E(0)$.
- **A1**: Correct value of $k = 12.5$.
- **M1**: Correct application of the chain rule for $E'(t)$.
- **A1**: Correct evaluation of $A'(30)$.
- **A1**: Final answer rounded to 3 sig figs.

steps.title: Find the initial amplitude
steps.content:
To find the value of $k$, we first need to determine the amplitude at $t = 0$. Using the provided model:

$$A(0) = 10 e^{-0.01(0)} + 8 \sin(0) - 6 \cos(0)$$

Since $e^0 = 1$, $\sin(0) = 0$, and $\cos(0) = 1$:

$$A(0) = 10(1) + 8(0) - 6(1) = 4 \text{ cm}$$

steps.title: Calculate the constant k
steps.content:
We are given that the stored energy at $t = 0$ is $200$ joules. We substitute $A(0) = 4$ and $E(0) = 200$ into the energy equation $$E(t) = k A(t)^2$$ to solve for $k$:

$$\begin{aligned} 200 &= k(4)^2 \\ 200 &= 16k \\ k &= \frac{200}{16} \\ k &= 12.5 \end{aligned}$$

steps.title: Define the energy function
steps.content:
Now that we have $k = 12.5$, the full energy function is:

$$E(t) = 12.5 \times \left[ 10 e^{-0.01 t} + 8 \sin \left(\frac{\pi}{30} t\right) - 6 \cos \left(\frac{\pi}{30} t\right) \right]^2$$

To find the rate of energy change at $t = 30$, we need the value of the derivative $E'(30)$. Since this is a complex function, we will use a Graphing Display Calculator (GDC) to find this numerical derivative directly.

**Note:** Ensure your calculator is in **Radian mode** whenever working with trigonometric functions in calculus.

steps.title: Identify the rate of change
steps.content:
In calculus, the "rate at which the population is increasing" is the derivative of the population function with respect to time. Therefore, we need to find the value of $f'(t)$ when $t = 10$, which is denoted as $f'(10)$.

This uses concepts from **Topic 5: Calculus**, specifically identifying derivatives as rates of change.

highlights.text: rate at which the population of fish is increasing at t = 10

steps.title: Apply the chain rule
steps.content:
To differentiate $f(t) = \frac{1000}{1 + 24e^{-0.2t}}$, it is often easier to rewrite it as a composite function with a negative exponent:

$$f(t) = 1000(1 + 24e^{-0.2t})^{-1}$$

Applying the chain rule, $\frac{d}{dt}[u^n] = nu^{n-1} \cdot \frac{du}{dt}$:

$$\begin{aligned} f'(t) &= 1000 \cdot (-1)(1 + 24e^{-0.2t})^{-2} \cdot \frac{d}{dt}(1 + 24e^{-0.2t}) \\ f'(t) &= -1000(1 + 24e^{-0.2t})^{-2} \cdot (24e^{-0.2t} \cdot -0.2) \\ f'(t) &= -1000(1 + 24e^{-0.2t})^{-2} \cdot (-4.8e^{-0.2t}) \\ f'(t) &= \frac{4800e^{-0.2t}}{(1 + 24e^{-0.2t})^2} \end{aligned}$$

This method follows the **AHL 5.9** syllabus requirement for differentiating composite functions.

steps.title: Substitute t = 10
steps.content:
Now, we substitute $t = 10$ into our derivative formula to find the specific rate of increase at that moment:

$$f'(10) = \frac{4800e^{-0.2(10)}}{(1 + 24e^{-0.2(10)})^2}$$

$$f'(10) = \frac{4800e^{-2}}{(1 + 24e^{-2})^2}$$
